demo-q-001: The correct answer is C (Femoral nerve). The femoral nerve (L2-L4) supplies the quadriceps femoris and is responsible for knee extension and the patellar reflex. Sciatic nerve injury would weaken hamstrings and dorsi/plantarflexion rather than the patellar reflex. Obturator innervates adductors; tibial is a branch of sciatic.
demo-q-002: The correct answer is B (Mitral valve). Rheumatic heart disease most commonly affects the mitral valve, with stenosis presenting as a mid-diastolic rumble at the apex with an opening snap. Aortic involvement is the next most common but usually causes regurgitation or stenosis murmurs rather than a diastolic rumble at the apex.
demo-q-003: The correct answer is A (Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency). G6PD deficiency causes oxidative hemolysis when patients are exposed to oxidative stressors such as primaquine, sulfa drugs, or fava beans. Heinz bodies and bite cells on peripheral smear are characteristic. Hereditary spherocytosis presents with chronic hemolysis without an oxidative trigger.
demo-q-004: The correct answer is D (Beta-lactamase production). Most ampicillin-resistant H. influenzae strains produce a TEM-1 beta-lactamase that hydrolyses the beta-lactam ring. The clinical workaround is a beta-lactamase inhibitor combination (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate) or a beta-lactamase-stable agent like a third-generation cephalosporin.
demo-q-005: The correct answer is B (Type II hypersensitivity). Goodpasture syndrome is caused by IgG autoantibodies directed against the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen in the glomerular and alveolar basement membranes — a classical antibody-mediated (Type II) reaction with linear immunofluorescence. Type III is immune complex (e.g., SLE); Type IV is T-cell mediated.
